title: Signed Tree Heads: Oban Worker and Public Verification Endpoint

story.i_want_to: fetch a current Signed Tree Head for a tenant's audit chain and verify it against the tenant's public audit key
story.so_that: I can cryptographically prove the chain's state at a specific position without trusting loopctl's running application, and I can detect divergence between my cached view and the server's current view

rationale: Hash-chained entries (US-26.1.1) are tamper-evident only if someone periodically commits the chain's state into a signed artifact that downstream verifiers can cache. Signed Tree Heads (STHs) — a pattern from Certificate Transparency and similar transparency log systems — give us that artifact: a signed tuple of (tenant_id, chain_position, merkle_root, timestamp). STHs are computed periodically by a background job and served via a public endpoint. The agent-witnessed verification in Phase 5 depends on STHs being available; this story ships the computation and serving infrastructure.

acceptance_criteria.description: Migration creates `audit_signed_tree_heads` table with columns: id (uuid PK), tenant_id (uuid FK), chain_position (bigint), merkle_root (bytea), signed_at (timestamptz), signature (bytea). Unique index on (tenant_id, chain_position). Index on (tenant_id, signed_at DESC).

`Loopctl.AuditChain.compute_merkle_root/1` takes a tenant_id and returns the SHA-256 merkle root of all audit_chain entry_hashes for that tenant, in chain_position order. Uses a straightforward pairwise merkle tree (hash(a || b) for pairs, handling odd count by doubling the last element).

`Loopctl.AuditChain.sign_tree_head/2` takes a tenant_id and a merkle_root, retrieves the tenant's private audit-signing key via `Loopctl.TenantKeys.get_private_key/1`, and produces an ed25519 signature over `tenant_id || chain_position || merkle_root || signed_at_unix`. Returns `{:ok, %{chain_position, merkle_root, signed_at, signature}}` or `{:error, reason}`.

Oban worker `Loopctl.Workers.ComputeSthWorker` is configured to run per tenant via two mechanisms: (a) scheduled every 60 seconds per tenant via Oban.Pro's cron or manual re-enqueue, (b) triggered inline when a tenant's chain gains 100+ new entries since the last STH. The worker is idempotent — running it twice in a row with no new entries is a no-op.

`GET /api/v1/audit/sth/:tenant_id` returns the latest STH for the tenant as JSON: `{tenant_id, chain_position, merkle_root (base64url), signed_at, signature (base64url)}`. No authentication required — the STH is designed for public verification.
`GET /api/v1/audit/sth/:tenant_id?at=<position>` returns the STH that covers the given chain position (the smallest STH with `chain_position >= at`). Returns 404 if no STH covers that position yet.

`mcp__loopctl__get_sth` MCP tool exposes the STH retrieval for agent consumption. Response includes the STH fields plus a helper note: `verification_hint: "Verify the signature against the tenant's audit_public_key from /api/v1/tenants/:id/audit_public_key"`.

A helper module `Loopctl.AuditChain.Verifier` provides `verify_sth/2` taking an STH and a public key and returning `{:ok, true} | {:error, :invalid_signature}`. This module is stateless and can be reused by the mcp-server (via Elixir→JS port or by reimplementation) for client-side verification.
Tenant isolation: cross-tenant STH requests return 404 (not 403). Tenant A's key cannot sign tenant B's STH. Confirmed via tests with two tenants and distinct keys.

Performance: computing a fresh merkle root for a chain of 10,000 entries takes under 100ms in benchmark tests. Beyond 100,000 entries, the worker uses an incremental merkle tree update instead of recomputing from scratch (deferred to a follow-up if under the threshold).
OpenAPI spec updates: new `SignedTreeHead` schema. Operation specs for the new endpoints. CHANGELOG entry.

test_cases.name: Merkle root computation is deterministic and reproducible
expected_results: Both calls return the same 32-byte value
The value matches an independently computed merkle root

test_cases.name: Worker computes and stores a new STH
expected_results: signature verifies against the tenant's audit_public_key

test_cases.name: Worker is idempotent — no new STH when no new entries
preconditions: tenant with 1 STH already at the current position
steps: Enqueue and run the worker again

test_cases.name: GET /audit/sth/:tenant_id returns the latest STH
expected_results: Body contains tenant_id, chain_position, merkle_root, signed_at, signature
merkle_root and signature are base64url encoded

test_cases.name: Verifier.verify_sth accepts a valid STH and rejects a tampered one
preconditions: tenant with STH and known public key
steps: Call verify_sth with the STH and public key
Modify one byte of merkle_root and call again

test_cases.name: STH at position query returns the correct STH
expected_results: First request returns the STH at position 10 (smallest >= 7)
Second request returns 404 (no STH covers position 20)

technical_notes: Worker: lib/loopctl/workers/compute_sth_worker.ex using Oban.Worker with `unique: [fields: [:worker, :args], period: 30]` to prevent duplicate enqueues.
Scheduling: add to config/config.exs Oban cron entries: `{"* * * * *", ComputeSthWorker, args: %{mode: :all_tenants}}` runs every minute and enqueues per-tenant jobs.
Incremental merkle: defer until benchmark shows it's needed. 10K entries × 32 bytes × SHA-256 is ~50ms on modern hardware.
Signing: use `:public_key.sign(message, :eddsa, private_key, [:ed25519])`. The private key must be in the raw 32-byte form.
STH endpoint route: `get "/audit/sth/:tenant_id", AuditSthController, :show` in the public scope.
base64url encoding for binary fields: use `Base.url_encode64(data, padding: false)`.
Verifier module is stateless and PURE (no IO), so it can be called from anywhere and tested easily.
mcp-server tool: add `knowledge_get_sth` (or similar name) in a follow-up mcp-server update story. This story ships only the Elixir side.
